mnemonics.title: Standard File Descriptors
mnemonics.content: Remember as '012' = 'Standard In, Out, Err' - 0 is input (like reading), 1 is output (writing results), 2 is errors (writing problems). The order matches how you typically interact: first provide input, get output, see any errors.

mnemonics.title: System Call Execution Flow
mnemonics.content: USER calls → LIBRARY wraps → KERNEL mode (privileged) → HARDWARE performs operation → return to KERNEL → return to USER with result. Think: 'ULKH' for User Library Kernel Hardware.

mnemonics.title: Error Handling Pattern
mnemonics.content: When system calls fail, remember: ALWAYS check for -1 return, THEN check errno. Use perror() for quick debugging - it prints your message plus the errno description automatically.

acronyms.usage: Standard ensuring Unix-like systems provide compatible APIs; write POSIX-compliant code for portability across Linux, BSD, macOS.
Mechanisms (pipes, sockets, message queues) allowing processes to exchange data; essential for building complex Unix programs.
Marker indicating no more data to read; read() returns 0 on EOF, not an error.
Set of functions/definitions enabling programs to interact; system calls form the kernel API.
Kernel layer abstracting file operations; allows support for multiple file systems (ext4, NTFS, NFS) through common interface.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a building: User space is the lobby (accessible to everyone), Kernel space is the secure vault (authorized personnel only). System calls are like requesting items from the vault through a clerk (kernel interface).

Draw a two-layer diagram: Top = User Programs/Library Functions (fread, printf), Bottom = Kernel (file system, scheduler). An arrow crossing downward shows system call transition, upward shows return with result.
Each process has a table: Index 0→STDIN, 1→STDOUT, 2→STDERR, then 3+ for opened files. Think of it as an array where new open() calls allocate the next available slot.

analogies.analogy: System call = ordering food from the restaurant kitchen (requires kitchen to prepare). Library function = microwaving leftover pizza in your kitchen (already in your space). Kitchen = kernel, your home = user space.

Think of errno like a receptionist who notes WHY a request failed. When you call (system call) and get 'failed' (-1), you ask the receptionist (check errno) to know whether it was 'not found', 'no permission', or 'disk full'.

The kernel is like the captain of a ship managing all operations (navigation, engine, cargo). Crew (user programs) cannot directly access the engine - they must request through the captain. This prevents chaos and ensures safety.

File descriptors are like table numbers in a restaurant. When you 'open' a file, you're assigned a table number (fd). You 'read' by ordering from that table, 'write' by giving instructions to that table. When done, 'close' frees the table.
